title: Your First Physics Experiment: Motion and Forces
description: Explore motion, forces, and energy through simple hands-on experiments using everyday objects. This session spans kinematics, Newton's laws, and energy conservation.

steps.instruction: Stack books to make a ramp (about 30° incline). Roll a ball down it and measure the time from release to bottom using a phone timer. Read about motion kinematics: what is acceleration and how does it relate to time?
steps.expectedOutcome: You observe that the ball speeds up as it rolls down, confirming that an unbalanced force (gravity) causes acceleration
steps.hint: Acceleration means the velocity is changing — the ball goes faster each second as gravity acts on it

steps.instruction: Push a book across a table with a gentle constant push. Notice: what happens when you stop pushing? Read about Newton's laws: which law explains why the book decelerates after you stop pushing?
steps.expectedOutcome: You identify Newton's First Law (objects in motion stay in motion unless acted on by a net force) and recognize friction as the unbalanced force stopping the book
steps.hint: On a frictionless surface the book would continue forever — friction is the force opposing motion

steps.instruction: Hold a heavy book and a pencil at the same height and drop them simultaneously. Read about gravitational acceleration: why do objects of different masses fall at the same rate?
steps.expectedOutcome: You confirm (approximately) that both objects hit the floor at the same time and understand that gravitational acceleration is constant regardless of mass
steps.hint: Galileo confirmed this — air resistance causes differences, not gravity; in vacuum all objects fall identically

steps.instruction: Stack books to the maximum height possible without tipping. Now lift one book from the top to the same height off the floor. Read about potential and kinetic energy: where is the energy stored in a raised object?
steps.expectedOutcome: You understand that lifting an object against gravity stores gravitational potential energy, which converts to kinetic energy when released
steps.hint: PE = mgh — the higher the object and the heavier it is, the more potential energy stored

steps.instruction: Rub two rough surfaces together. Feel the heat generated. Read about work and thermodynamics: where did the energy of your motion go? Is energy destroyed?
steps.expectedOutcome: You understand that friction converts mechanical energy to thermal energy — energy is conserved (not destroyed) but becomes less useful
steps.hint: Energy conservation: the total energy before = total energy after. Friction converts ordered motion into disordered heat
